text: The surgeon manipulates the preperitoneal flap, encountering adhesion near the infraepigastric vessels. Efforts are made to create space and reorient the flap to continue the dissection.
The surgeon carefully pulls the pocket away from the abdominal wall, noting the minimal presence of lateral fat to avoid drifting into the abdominal wall.
The surgeon operates within the supravesicular space, maneuvering instruments back and forth to manipulate the tissue in this area.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to a right lateral oblique angle, enhancing the view for differentiation. Using electrocautery, the surgeon buzzes directly on the hernia sac, pulling surrounding structures and fat to allow them to fade and fall away.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz directly on the hernia sac, pushing surrounding fat laterally to expose the edge of the sac. The vas deferens becomes visible, and the surgeon extracts a small cord lipoma accompanying it.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens on the right side, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens up the fat layer to orient themselves towards the Cooper's ligament and pubic tubercles, revealing underlying anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon visually identifies the edge of the sac and searches for a cord lipoma within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the corner, ensuring the dissection remains distant from the iliac vessels, while maintaining focus on the surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon performs bilateral suprapubic fixation, with high medial positioning. The muscles are visible in front of the surgical field, and there are no identifiable vascular structures obstructing the view.
